summary: A broad automatic-permuter pass over high-percent functions made many small source-shape changes across fighter, item, effect, game-mode, interface, stage, library, menu, and toy code. The bot report credited the PR with +6896 matched-code bytes, +20 matched-data bytes, and 50 new matches, including Effect_RemoveUserData, ftCo_80097F38, several Kirby SpecialN-related functions, gmCamera_801A2798, un_802FD9D8, and multiple item/UI helpers. The largest structural edit was the removal of a very large block from ftKb_Init.c after ftKb_SpecialN_800F1F68, while most other hunks used regalloc/ordering tactics such as temporary variables, direct HSD_GObjGetUserData calls, comma operators, variable declaration reordering, and tiny inline wrappers.
what_changed: Ran a short permuter sweep on high-percent functions; the PR body states: "Ran permuter on all high % functions (only for 1 minute tbh), but this is what I got".
Matched-code progress increased by +6896 bytes and matched-data by +20 bytes according to the decomp.dev bot report.

Produced 50 new matches; examples from the bot report include un_802FD9D8, ftKb_SpecialN_800F15D8, un_80306C5C, gmCamera_801A2798, it_802E628C, ftCo_80097F38, it_80275788, lb_8001B99C, Effect_RemoveUserData, and gm_8016ECE8.

Removed a huge trailing block from src/melee/ft/chara/ftKirby/ftKb_Init.c: the file changed by +4/-7962 lines, with the diff deleting many functions and stubs after ftKb_SpecialN_800F1F68, including ftKb_AttackDash_* and many Kirby SpecialHi/SpecialN handlers.
Adjusted Effect_RemoveUserData in eflib.c with an inline helper returning &Effect_AllocData, extra temporaries, and an empty do-while block before decrementing efLib_804D64EC.
Changed ftCo_80097F38 to store the DownDamageU ternary motion-state result in a local int before calling Fighter_ChangeMotionState.

Changed several Kirby hunks to use HSD_GObjGetUserData(gobj) directly instead of GET_FIGHTER(gobj), or to add temporaries around Fighter pointers, callback assignments, item pointers, hat-table accesses, NULL assignments, and constants.
Used matching-only expression-shape tricks in multiple places, including comma operators such as (0, fp->co_attrs.air_drift_max), (0, GET_FIGHTER(gobj)), and ((0, 0)).
Made small header/prototype formatting and parameter-name edits, including gm_1601.h line wrapping and gmresult.h naming fn_80174920's ResultsPlayerData pointer parameter as data.
smart_moves: Targeted functions already near 100%, where small source-shape permutations can cheaply finish matches; the bot report shows many items moving from roughly 85-99% to 100%.

Accepted localized, low-semantic-risk regalloc changes: temporary variables, declaration order changes, direct userdata fetches, and inline wrapper functions alter compiler output without changing intended runtime behavior.
Used function-local inline helpers for matching where the compiler needed a different call/expression shape, as seen with Effect_RemoveUserData_inline and ftKb_MtSpecialAirNCancel_Anim_inline.
Kept autogenerated/permuter artifacts narrowly scoped to matching hunks rather than attempting broad renames or semantic rewrites in this PR.
Let the decomp.dev bot quantify the impact; the report documented both wins and the small unmatched .sdata regression in ftKb_Init.
decomp_lessons: For high-percent functions, small regalloc nudges can be enough: assigning ternary results, constants, callback function pointers, or table lookups to locals can finish a match.
GET_FIGHTER(gobj) versus HSD_GObjGetUserData(gobj) is not just stylistic in matching work; the diff shows direct HSD_GObjGetUserData calls used to alter generated code.
Tiny no-op constructs such as empty do-while blocks and comma operators can be valid matching tools, but they should remain isolated and evidence-backed because they reduce readability.
Inline wrappers returning an argument or global address can influence inlining/call structure enough to match, as shown in eflib.c and ftKb_SpecialNZd.c.

Large file-split or duplicate-block cleanup can affect data matching significantly; ftKb_Init.c had a very large deletion and the bot reported a +1545-byte .data improvement but a -23-byte .sdata regression.

Header-only edits may be part of matching passes when prototype shape, parameter names, signedness, or formatting interacts with declarations or generated code evidence, but this PR provides limited explanation beyond the diff.
naming_conventions: Unknown functions remained in project style as fn_801xxxxx or subsystem-prefixed symbols such as ftKb_SpecialN_800F15D8, gm_8016ECE8, and un_802FD9D8.
The permuter introduced generic temporary names such as new_var and new_var2; these are matching artifacts rather than descriptive naming improvements.

Subsystem prefixes were preserved: ftKb for Kirby fighter code, ftCo for common fighter code, efLib for effects, gm/gmCamera for game mode and camera, it for item code, if/un for interface/unknown UI functions.
gmresult.h changed a prototype from an unnamed ResultsPlayerData pointer to struct ResultsPlayerData* data, showing the local convention of naming prototype parameters when useful.
No reviewer-driven naming guidance was present in the available comments.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Introduce locals for expression results before calls, e.g. ftCo_80097F38 assigns the DownWait motion ID to new_var before Fighter_ChangeMotionState.
Replace macro-style fighter access with HSD_GObjGetUserData(gobj) to alter casts/register allocation.
Store callback symbols such as efLib_PauseAll and efLib_ResumeAll in temporaries before assigning Fighter callback fields.
Use comma operators to force expression codegen shape, with examples in Kirby SpecialN/SpecialNZd and ft_0CD1 hunks.
Use empty do { } while (0) and tiny inline helper functions to perturb compiler output while preserving behavior.
Reorder local declarations, such as gm_8016ECE8 moving int count after int i, to match stack/register allocation.
Split compound assignments or modulo expressions into temporaries, e.g. gmCamera_801A2798 stores x14 + 1 before applying % 2.
Use redundant masks or no-op increments/decrements in very small functions where the permuter found a matching code shape, e.g. if_2F72.c and if_2FC93.c.
review_feedback: No human review comments were present in the provided slice.
The decomp.dev bot reported GALE01 progress from ff7f858 to c5fb880: matched code 54.47% (+0.18%, +6896 bytes), matched data 20.95% (+0.00%, +20 bytes).
Bot report listed 50 new matches and 2 improvements in unmatched items.
Bot report also noted 1 regression in an unmatched item: main/melee/ft/chara/ftKirby/ftKb_Init .sdata decreased by 23 bytes, from 19.55% to 0.00%.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that clean up generic permuter temporaries named new_var or new_var2 from PR 2200.
Check whether the ftKb_Init.c .sdata regression reported by the bot was fixed in a later PR.
Search for subsequent work around ftKb_SpecialN_800F15D8, ftKb_SpecialN_800EFF9C, and Kirby SpecialN files to see whether the matching artifacts were replaced with clearer code.
Look for other PRs using HSD_GObjGetUserData instead of GET_FIGHTER as a matching tactic.
Audit Effect_RemoveUserData_inline and ftKb_MtSpecialAirNCancel_Anim_inline for later cleanup or preservation.
